name: swift-comet-pipeline

metadata.name: swift_comet_pipeline
metadata.summary: Data reduction pipeline for determining water production rates from the Swift telescope's UVOT images

documentation:
# swift_comet_pipeline
A package to analyze UVOT image data of comets from the Swift satellite (https://www.swift.ac.uk/).  A related project at https://github.com/sjoset/swift_portal_downloader automates the acquisition of data appropriate for this pipeline.

## Development installation:
## Install poetry
https://www.python-poetry.org

## Create conda environment with python
```
    conda create --name env_name_here python=3.11
    conda activate env_name_here
```

## Clone this repository

## Install package locally
In the repository's directory:
```
    poetry install
```

### Configuration
Configuration is stored in a YAML file with the following entries to start a project:
```
    swift_data_path: /path/to/swift/data/
    project_path: /path/to/where/to/save/results
    jpl_horizons_id: String identifying comet that JPL Horizons can use to find comet orbital information
    vectorial_model_quality: String to select the calculation quality of the models used to determine water production: may be low, medium, high, or very_high
    vectorial_model_backend: [sbpy, rust]
```
By default the file **config.yaml** in the current directory is assumed unless specified otherwise on the command line.

An example config might look like this:
#### config.yaml
```
    swift_data_path: /Users/user/swift_data_downloads/c2013us10
    project_path: /Users/user/analysis_c2013us10
    jpl_horizons_id: C/2013 US10
    vectorial_model_quality: very_high
    vectorial_model_backend: sbpy
```

This holds all of the products associated with this sub-pipeline

documentation:
If any in the list is 'invalid', the return value is 'invalid'.
If all are 'complete', the return value is 'complete'.
If all are 'not_complate', the return value is 'not_complete'.
Otherwise, the return value is 'partial'.

classes.name: swift_comet_pipeline.pipeline.products.epoch_subpipeline.stacking_step.stacked_epoch_product.StackedEpochProduct
documentation:
These epochs have all DataFrame rows removed that were not included in image stacking, like images taken in non-UW1 or non-UVV filters.
This removes the need for logic to check if any particular dataframe row was included or not - we can read this epoch and assume every image mentioned is included.

documentation:
instead of fighting fits file formatting and HDU lists, we assert that hdu[0] is an empty primary HDU
and that hdu[1] is an ImageHDU, with relevant header and image data, for the images we create via
the pipeline

This is NOT a general-purpose FITS reader/writer!

classes.name: swift_comet_pipeline.pipeline.products.lightcurve.lightcurve_products.CompleteVectorialLightCurveProduct
documentation:
Contains production values resulting from different vectorial model fitting methods at varying dust redness
This is a concatenation of near, far, and full fits

columns:
observation_time, time_from_perihelion_days, rh_au, near_fit_q, near_fit_q_err, far_fit_q, far_fit_q_err, full_fit_q, full_fit_q_err, dust_redness

classes.name: swift_comet_pipeline.pipeline.products.lightcurve.lightcurve_products.BestRednessLightCurveProduct
documentation:
Contains production values resulting from the specified fitting method, using values that result from a dust redness that
gives the lowest percent error in the production

columns:
observation_time, time_from_perihelion_days, rh_au, q, q_err, dust_redness

classes.name: swift_comet_pipeline.pipeline.products.lightcurve.lightcurve_products.ApertureLightCurveProduct
documentation: Contains production values as a function of aperture radius at varying dust redness at every epoch, with the production being averages over all of the plateaus found at that redness
TODO: We have to pick the near, far, or full fit (or all of them) to apply bayesian dust analysis - this isn't enough!
Holds the result of taking CompleteVectorialLightCurveProduct and appyling various dust redness priors

classes.name: swift_comet_pipeline.pipeline.products.lightcurve.lightcurve_products.BayesianApertureLightCurveProduct
documentation: Contains production values at various dust redness priors at every epoch: it is a further-processed version of the ApertureLightCurveProduct

documentation: avg_of_means = np.mean([x.mean for x in walked_aperture_stats])
avg_of_medians = np.mean([x.median for x in walked_aperture_stats])
weights = [x.r**2 for x in walked_apertures]  # type: ignore
median_mu = np.average([x.median for x in walked_aperture_stats], weights=weights)
largest_ap_idx = np.argmax([x.r for x in walked_apertures])  # type: ignore

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.post_pipeline_analysis.vectorial_fitting_reliable.vectorial_fitting_reliable
documentation:
For vectorial fitting to be reliable, it needs to have information covering roughly min_distance_coverage (100,000 km),
and the profile must extend beyond num_psfs_required (default 3), otherwise the column density is too smeared by the psf

documentation:
lc_df should be a lightcurve converted to a dataframe with lightcurve_to_dataframe()

NOTE: This function rounds the time to the nearest day, so if there are two epochs very close to one another,
this may cause an issue.  A warning is printed in this case, but the dataframe is returned with no errors.

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.image_manipulation.image_pad.pad_to_match_sizes
documentation:
Given two images, zero-pad the edges of the smaller image so that both images end up the same width and
have an odd number of columns.
Does the same for the height, guaranteeing on odd number of rows in the resulting images.
Preserves the center of each image, so that the new images only have new (zero) pixels at their edges.

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.stacking.stacking.determine_stacking_image_size
documentation:
Opens every FITS file specified in the given epoch and finds the image size necessary to accommodate
the largest image involved in the stack, so we can pad out the smaller images and stack them in one step

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.stacking.stacking.stack_epoch_into_sum_and_median
documentation:
Blindly takes every entry in the given Epoch and attempts to stack it - epoch should be pre-filtered because
no checks are made here
If successful, returns a tuple of images: (sum, median, exposure_map)
The exposure_map image has pixels with values in units of seconds - the total exposure time from the stack of images involved

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.stacking.stacking.make_uw1_and_uvv_stacks
documentation:
Produces sum- and median-stacked images for the uw1 and uvv filters
The stacked images are padded so that the images in uw1 and uvv are the same size, so both must be stacked here

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.stacking.stacking.write_uw1_and_uvv_stacks
documentation:
Writes the stacked epoch dataframe, along with the four images created during stacking, and exposure map
This is a separate step so that the stacking results can be viewed before deciding to save or not save the results

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.image_manipulation.image_recenter.get_image_dimensions_to_center_on_pixel
documentation:
If we want to re-center the source_image on coords_to_center, we need to figure out the dimensions the new image
needs to be to fit the old picture after we shift it over to its new position
Returns tuple of (rows, columns) the new image would have to be

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.image_manipulation.image_recenter.center_image_on_coords
documentation: Takes a source_image and pads with zeros so that the new image has source_coords_to_center

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.aperture.q_vs_aperture_radius_seaborn.show_q_vs_aperture_radius_seaborn
documentation:
Shows a scatterplot of log(q_h2o) vs aperture radius in km, with all data combined and colored according to the
assumed dust color.  Also indicates plateau concentrations by drawing a vspan at a low alpha value where a plateau
occurs, leading to shades where the plateaus from different rednesses agree.

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.aperture.aperture_count_rate.aperture_count_rate
documentation:
Constructs a circular aperture of aperture_radius at aperture_center and takes the sum of pixels
inside as the count rate, along with its error

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.aperture.q_vs_aperture_kde_seaborn.show_q_density_estimates_vs_redness
documentation:
Takes the results in list q_vs_aperture_radius_list and computes a histogram of Q(h2o) values that appear at each dust_redness, plots
each histogram in its own row, and highlights the production values where a plateau was detected

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.aperture.plateau_serialize.dust_plateau_list_dict_serialize

documentation: Make a gaussian psf, returned as a 2d array, with the gaussian peak in the middle of the array in both dimensions

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.swift.uvot_image.get_uvot_image_center_row_col
documentation: Given a SwiftUVOTImage, returns the (row, column) of the center pixel

classes.name: swift_comet_pipeline.swift.uvot_image.SwiftImageDataMode
documentation: Maps the strings in FITS file header under the keyword DATAMODE

classes.name: swift_comet_pipeline.swift.uvot_image.SwiftPixelResolution
documentation: The pixel resolution of the given modes according to the swift documentation

classes.name: swift_comet_pipeline.swift.uvot_image.PixelCoord
documentation: Use floats instead of ints to allow sub-pixel addressing if we need

classes.name: swift_comet_pipeline.swift.swift_data.SwiftData

methods.name: swift_comet_pipeline.swift.swift_data.SwiftData.get_all_observation_ids
documentation:
build a list of folders in the swift data directory, filtering any directories
that don't match the naming structure of 11 numerical digits, and returns a list of every observation id found

methods.name: swift_comet_pipeline.swift.swift_data.SwiftData.get_all_orbit_ids
documentation: build a list of orbit ids based on the folder names in the swift data directory

methods.name: swift_comet_pipeline.swift.swift_data.SwiftData.get_swift_uvot_image_paths
documentation:
Given an observation ID, filter type, and image type, returns a list of FITS files that match.
Some observations have multiple files using the same filter, so we have to do it this way

methods.name: swift_comet_pipeline.swift.swift_data.SwiftData.get_uvot_image_directory
documentation: Returns a path to the directory containing the uvot images of the given observation id

Class that takes a directory that points to Swift data, which is assumed to be in this format:
    data_path/
        [observation id]/
            uvot/
                image/
                    sw[observation id][filter]_[image type].img.gz

Then given an observation id, filter, and image type, we can construct the path to this file

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.comet.comet_center.invalid_user_center_value
documentation: Pixel coordinate value stored in the observation log when the user has not manually adjusted the comet center for an image

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.comet.comet_center.has_valid_user_center_value
documentation:
Takes an observation log dataframe row or Epoch dataframe row and returns if
the user has specified a comet center or not in the veto pipeline step

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.comet.comet_center.get_horizons_comet_center
documentation:
Takes an observation log dataframe row or Epoch dataframe row and returns a PixelCoord of the comet center
as specified by JPL Horizons

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.comet.comet_center.get_user_specified_comet_center
documentation:
Takes an observation log dataframe row or Epoch dataframe row and returns either a PixelCoord of the comet
center the user specified in the veto step, or returns None

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.comet.comet_center.get_comet_center_prefer_user_coords
documentation:
Takes an observation log dataframe row or Epoch dataframe row and returns a PixelCoord of the comet center

If a user-specified comet center exists, we prefer that over the JPL Horizons value
If no user-specified comet center exists, return the JPL Horizons value

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.comet.comet_radial_profile.extract_comet_radial_profile
documentation:
Extracts the count rate profile along a line starting at the comet center, extending out a distance r at angle theta
Takes one pixel sample per unit distance: if r=100, we take 101 pixel samples (to include the center pixel)
It is important to sample one pixel per r as the processing we do later relies on profile[x] being sampled at radius=x

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.comet.comet_radial_profile.extract_comet_radial_median_profile_from_cone
documentation: Take a profile of radius r at angle theta, and use profiles from theta +/- cone_size to calculate a median pixel value at each radius

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.comet.comet_radial_profile.count_rate_from_comet_radial_profile
documentation:
Takes a radial profile and assumes azimuthal symmetry to produce a count rate that would result
from a circular aperture centered on the comet profile
Reminder: we need the background count rate to propogate error

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.comet.comet_radial_profile.calculate_distance_from_center_mesh
documentation:
Resulting array has the same dimensions as the input img, but the pixel values are now the distance to the center of the image,
rounded to the nearest integer.  This allows addressing a radial profile array with the mesh as the index: radial_profile[distance_from_center_mesh]

This works if we sample the radial profile at r = 0, r = 1, r = 2, ... but will break if we choose to sample differently

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.comet.comet_radial_profile.radial_profile_to_image
documentation:
The array distance_from_center_mesh is assumed to be a 2d array, whose values express the distance from that pixel to the center of the image, rounded to the nearest integer.
To generate the image easily, we zero-pad the comet's radial profile out to the maximum distance specified in this mesh (if necessary)

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.comet.comet_radial_profile.radial_profile_to_dataframe_product
documentation: Takes a radial profile and returns a dataframe with metadata attached that can be written to disk as a PipelineProduct

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.comet.comet_radial_profile.radial_profile_from_dataframe_product
documentation: Reads a saved PipelineProduct we stored with radial_profile_from_dataframe_product and reconstructs a radial profile

classes.name: swift_comet_pipeline.comet.comet_radial_profile.CometRadialProfile
documentation:
Count rate values along a line extending from the comet center out to radius r at angle theta
Theta is measured from the positive x-axis (along a numpy row) counter-clockwise
One sample is taken of the profile per unit radial distance: If we want a cut of radius 20, we will have 20
(x, y) pairs sampled.  We will also have the comet center at radius zero for a total of 21 points in the resulting profile.

The stacking step adjusts the image pixels to be in count rate, so pixel_values will be an array of floats representing count rates

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.comet.comet_surface_brightness_profile.countrate_profile_to_surface_brightness
documentation:
CometCountRateProfile holds the count rates, with countrate_profile[x] being the count rate of radius = x pixels
SwiftPixelResolution holds the number of arcseconds per pixel

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.comet.calculate_column_density.surface_brightness_profile_to_column_density
documentation: TODO: add decorators to enforce the arguments are the correct Quantity

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.comet.comet_profile_fitting.fit_comet_profile_gaussian
documentation: Takes a CometRadialProfile and returns a function f(r) generated from the profile's best-fit gaussian

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.comet.comet_radius.estimate_comet_radius_at_angle
documentation: TODO: re-write to take a pre-generated CometRadialProfile, and possibly the background information,
and then test when the signal stays at < 1 sigma background for a certain number of pixels:
maybe a percentage of the radius being considered?  Look ahead the next percent * r pixels and test?

classes.name: swift_comet_pipeline.comet.comet_profile.CometProfile

methods.name: swift_comet_pipeline.comet.comet_profile.CometProfile.from_radial_profile
documentation: Mirror the given radial profile about the center of the comet, simulating slice of size r along theta with slice of size r along -theta
CometRadialProfile can be assumed to extend outward from the center of the comet, while this structure is intended for any arbitrary profile of pixel values

functions.name: swift_comet_pipeline.comet.comet_center_finding.find_comet_center
documentation: Coordinates returned are x, y values
